input_path: data\papers\Heithoff et al. - A Digital Shadow for Accurate Robot Motion Control Integrating Data with Friction Models.pdf

extracted_characteristics.tooling_and_enablers: The Digital Twin system employs Model-Based Systems Engineering (MBSE) methodologies including SysML for system modeling, with RFLP (Requirements, Functions, Logics, Physics), OOSEM, SPES, and SMArDT for handling multi-level abstraction (requirements, functional architecture, logical architecture, product/technical layer). Architecture Description Languages (ADLs) such as ACME, AADL, MontiArc, and SysML IBDs are utilized for component & connector-based system descriptions, detailing components, interfaces, interconnections, and composition. Behavior modeling is supported via MontiArc Automaton and SysML v1.7 state machines. Additional tooling includes MathWorks Simulink for simulation, general-purpose programming languages (C++, Java), and other simulation tools referenced in [29], [30], enabling integration of heterogeneous stakeholder perspectives and modeling notations.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_time_scale: The digital shadow operates with real-time, data-driven representations utilizing live sensor data streams for continuous identification of physical system states, ensuring one-way data flow synchronization between physical and digital components without bidirectional communication.
extracted_characteristics.dt_constellation: The Digital Shadow Reference Model defines a UML class diagram structure with components including Asset (representing physical entities), Processing (handling data transformation via calculation or simulation models), and Model (engineering or simulation models). The system coordinates these components through traceability links where data traces originate from processing steps, enabling fine-grained traceability across the shadow's architecture.
extracted_characteristics.horizontal_integration: Integration with external systems is achieved via Asset Administration Shells (AAS) for standardized interoperability and MQTT protocol for streaming sensor data from external controllers, ensuring seamless data exchange between the digital shadow and cyber-physical system components.
extracted_characteristics.dt_models_and_data: The system utilizes Model-Based Systems Engineering (MBSE) with SysML for structural modeling (IBDs), MontiArc Automaton and SysML state machines for behavioral modeling, and MathWorks Simulink for analytical simulations. Data sources are derived from physical system measurements via one-way data flows, aggregated and abstracted for specific purposes using methodologies like RFLP, OOSEM, SPES, and SMArDT. Model relationships are defined through component interfaces and connectors in ADLs such as ACME and AADL.
extracted_characteristics.physical_acting_components: Physical actuators include a permanent magnet synchronous motor driving the first axis, with gear transmission and cycloidal gear mechanisms. Control is implemented via the Siemens SINUMERIK 840D sl system, which processes inputs such as position, speed, and acceleration commands. The actuation system adheres to safety constraints and precise addressing mechanisms for each axis, ensuring accurate motion control within specified operational limits.
extracted_characteristics.fidelity_and_validity_considerations: Model fidelity is maintained through the integration of analytical and data-driven models with live physical data to enhance accuracy. Validation involves comparing reference values against simulated outputs and quantifying deviations between measured and simulated data. Traceability of data to its origin and computational methods is enforced, with systematic error detection and correction mechanisms embedded in the verification process to ensure reliability.
extracted_characteristics.physical_to_virtual_interaction: An automated one-way data flow from physical to virtual systems, transmitting time-series sensor data (e.g., input current and output angle) via MQTT protocol with real-time updates for state synchronization.
extracted_characteristics.virtual_to_physical_interaction: No virtual-to-physical interaction occurs, as the digital shadow functions as a passive entity that neither operates autonomously nor pushes data back to the physical system.
extracted_characteristics.dt_technical_connection: MQTT protocol is used for sensor data streaming from physical to digital components, with data visualization in a dashboard. No details on network topology, bandwidth, latency, reliability, redundancy, or security protocols are provided.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_process_and_dt_evolution: The engineering process employs Model-Based Systems Engineering (MBSE) methodologies including RFLP (Requirements, Functions, Logics, Physics), OOSEM, SPES, and SMArDT for system complexity management. Architecture Description Languages (ADLs) such as ACME, AADL, MontiArc, and SysML IBDs define component & connector structures, with behavior specified via MontiArc Automaton or SysML state machines. Models are integrated with MathWorks Simulink, simulation tools, and general-purpose programming languages (C++, Java) for detailed behavior description.
extracted_characteristics.standardization: Compliance with industry standards including SysML for systems engineering modeling, AADL for architecture description, ACME for component-based modeling, and MontiArc for architecture modeling, facilitating interoperability across heterogeneous stakeholders and tools as part of MBSE practices.

II. B ACKGROUND

To better understand our approach, we describe relevant
modeling methods, important concepts for DSs, and methods
on how to derive concrete DSs.


_A. Modeling Software and Systems_

To describe the structure and behavior of systems, ModelBased Systems-Engineering (MBSE) uses systems engineering
modeling languages such as SysML [17] during the development phase. Due to the complexity of systems, modeling
is typically split into different levels of abstraction, e.g.,
requirements, functional architecture, logical architecture, and
product/technical layer. There exist different methodologies
on how to handle this complexity, e.g., RFLP (Requirements, Functions, Logics, Physics) [18], [19], OOSEM [20],
SPES [21], or SMArDT [22]. To describe the functional
and logical architecture, typically architecture description languages (ADLs) are used. As main concepts, they describe
systems from a component & connector perspective, e.g.,
ACME [23], AADL [24], MontiArc [25], [26] or SysML
IBDs [17], [27]. These typically describe the system’s components, their interfaces, their interconnections, and composition.
Some ADLs also include methods to describe the system’s
behavior, e.g., MontiArc Automaton or state machines in
SysML v1.7. To describe behavior in more detail, these models
are often combined with further (modeling) tools such as
MathWorks Simulink [28], other simulation tools and methods [29], [30], or code in general-purpose languages like C++
or Java.

Thus, a comprehensive description of a system from an
MBSE perspective might have to cope with models in different
modeling notations and different tools. In addition, various
heterogeneous stakeholders and perspectives need to be integrated [31].


_B. Digital Shadow Reference Model_

Kritzinger et al. [32] differentiate digital shadows from
digital twins based on their communication flow. A digital
shadow is characterized by “ _an automated one-way data_
_flow between the state of an existing physical object and_
_a digital object_ [32]”. In practical terms, this means that a
digital shadow functions as a passive entity: it neither operates
autonomously nor pushes data back to the physical system.
Therefore, software systems require active components to
interpret and process this incoming data [33], [34]. A Digital
Twin (DT) can provide such active components [35]–[37].
In the remainder of this paper, we understand a digital
shadow as _“a set of contextual data traces and their ag-_
_gregation and abstraction collected for a specific purpose_



**Fig. 1:** Digital shadow reference model as a UML class
diagram. It defines important concepts for digital shadows in
manufacturing.

block_1_judge.reasoning: The extraction states 'Not in Document', but the source text explicitly describes the physical system under study. The system is identified as a 'serial robot MABI MAX 100' with specific components like the 'first vertical robot axis', 'motor', 'gearbox', and 'position encoders'. It is used for 'machining' and manufacturing tasks. Because evidence is present in the text, this is an incorrect extraction.

The extraction states 'Not in Document', but the source text describes specific functionalities and purposes of the digital shadow that constitute services. These include 'visualization in a dashboard', 'continuous... identification of joint friction', and improving 'robot’s motion accuracy'. These map directly to service types like visualization and monitoring defined in the characteristic description.

The extraction accurately summarizes the tools and methodologies described in the 'Modeling Software and Systems' section of the background. It correctly identifies 'Model-Based Systems-Engineering (MBSE)' and 'SysML' as primary enablers, and lists 'MathWorks Simulink' for simulation. While the text presents other methodologies like RFLP, OOSEM, ACME, and AADL as general examples ('e.g.') rather than explicitly confirming their usage in this specific implementation, the extraction captures the technical context and tooling landscape provided in the source text.

modeling methods, important concepts for DSs, and methods
on how to derive concrete DSs.


_A. Modeling Software and Systems_

To describe the structure and behavior of systems, ModelBased Systems-Engineering (MBSE) uses systems engineering
modeling languages such as SysML [17] during the development phase. Due to the complexity of systems, modeling
is typically split into different levels of abstraction, e.g.,
requirements, functional architecture, logical architecture, and
product/technical layer. There exist different methodologies
on how to handle this complexity, e.g., RFLP (Requirements, Functions, Logics, Physics) [18], [19], OOSEM [20],
SPES [21], or SMArDT [22]. To describe the functional
and logical architecture, typically architecture description languages (ADLs) are used. As main concepts, they describe
systems from a component & connector perspective, e.g.,
ACME [23], AADL [24], MontiArc [25], [26] or SysML
IBDs [17], [27]. These typically describe the system’s components, their interfaces, their interconnections, and composition.
Some ADLs also include methods to describe the system’s
behavior, e.g., MontiArc Automaton or state machines in
SysML v1.7. To describe behavior in more detail, these models
are often combined with further (modeling) tools such as
MathWorks Simulink [28], other simulation tools and methods [29], [30], or code in general-purpose languages like C++
or Java.

Thus, a comprehensive description of a system from an
MBSE perspective might have to cope with models in different
modeling notations and different tools. In addition, various
heterogeneous stakeholders and perspectives need to be integrated [31].


_B. Digital Shadow Reference Model_

Kritzinger et al. [32] differentiate digital shadows from
digital twins based on their communication flow. A digital
shadow is characterized by “ _an automated one-way data_
_flow between the state of an existing physical object and_
_a digital object_ [32]”. In practical terms, this means that a
digital shadow functions as a passive entity: it neither operates
autonomously nor pushes data back to the physical system.
Therefore, software systems require active components to
interpret and process this incoming data [33], [34]. A Digital
Twin (DT) can provide such active components [35]–[37].
In the remainder of this paper, we understand a digital
shadow as _“a set of contextual data traces and their ag-_
_gregation and abstraction collected for a specific purpose_



**Fig. 1:** Digital shadow reference model as a UML class
diagram. It defines important concepts for digital shadows in
manufacturing.

nents. Additional Metadata supports further data processing, particularly in digital twin scenarios. Different types of
models are used depending on the goal: engineering models
describe the system’s structure, properties, or behaviors, while
simulation and data calculation models help compute higherlevel abstractions. A Processing component performs these
computational steps: it consumes (uses) input data traces and
produces output data, which then forms part of the shadow.
The processing logic is encoded in either data calculation or
simulation models, supporting fine-grained traceability [16].
By following the originates from association, one can
trace a data trace back to its generating processing component,
the consumed input traces, and the models involved. If a data
trace is computed using other traces, this traceability remains
intact in a transitive manner.

This DS structure is described as a reference model on

purpose: A digital shadow designer has the flexibility to
concretize the Digital Shadow Reference Model and customize
the DS model to suit their specific use case, as we do in this
paper. _Reference models_ describe concepts of a domain and
their relationships, and they have “ _a conformance relation that_
_precisely identifies the set of conforming models within the_
_same language” [41]_ . In a concretization development step,
one can come up with such a _conforming_ model (a _concrete_
_realization_ or _concretization_ ) for a reference model. Such a
concrete realization _incarnates_ the concepts from the reference model using concrete elements and relations. For Class
Diagrams (CDs), we specify classes and their associations,
conforming to the reference CD, either by choosing similar
names or giving a concrete _conformance relation_, e.g., in the
form of a mapping of incarnations to reference concepts.


_C. How to create Digital Shadows_


To define digital shadows requires collecting all relevant
information for their constituents [38], e.g., which concrete
data traces are needed from which part of a cyber-physical
system (CPS). Within [15], we have sketched a method on how
to create DSs, further refined it with use cases from injection
molding in [42], and integrated it in a method to define digital
twins [43].
There are two ways of designing digital shadows:. The first
option is to come up from the data which is accessible from
the system. The second option is purpose-driven, where we
start from the particular reason and requirements we aim to
create the DS for. The main steps are:

block_2_judge.reasoning: The extraction correctly identifies the digital shadow as real-time and data-driven, supported by the quote 'real-time, data-driven representations of robot joint behavior'. It accurately describes the one-way data flow synchronization, citing the definition 'an automated one-way data flow between the state of an existing physical object and a digital object'. However, it adds 'without bidirectional communication' as a mechanism for synchronization, which is an inference rather than an explicit description of the synchronization mechanism in the text, though the definition implies it. The extraction is well-grounded but slightly interpretive.

The description states 'Not in Document', which is accurate. The text focuses on a single instance, noting that 'FrictionShadow acts as the identification element for one digital shadow' for the specific scope of the robot's first axis, and does not discuss multiple instances or hierarchical twin structures.

The extraction accurately describes the Digital Shadow Reference Model (DSRM) components, including Asset, Processing, and Model (differentiated into Engineering, Data Calculation, and Simulation), as well as the UML class diagram structure. It correctly explains the traceability links and coordination. This is strongly supported by quotes: 'Fig. 1 presents this refined version of the Digital Shadow Reference Model (DSRM) as a Unified Modeling Language (UML) class diagram' and 'A key refinement... is the differentiation of models into three types: Engineering models, Data Calculation models, and Simulation models... This facilitates traceability of how data within a DS has been generated.'

The extraction correctly identifies Asset Administration Shells (AAS) and MQTT as integration standards/protocols. The text mentions 'implementing Asset Administration Shells (AAS), and live-data linking' and 'sensors... are streamed via MQTT'. However, the extraction specifies MQTT is for 'streaming sensor data from external controllers', while the text only mentions 'sensors' and a 'Siemens SINUMERIK 840D sl' controller without explicitly linking MQTT to the controller or labeling the controller as 'external'. The extraction is accurate but makes a minor unsupported inference about the external controller.

block_3_judge.reasoning: The extraction is highly detailed and accurately reflects the source text. It correctly identifies MBSE with SysML for structural modeling (IBDs), mentions MontiArc Automaton and SysML state machines for behavior, and MathWorks Simulink for analytical simulations. It also correctly cites the one-way data flow characteristic of digital shadows ('automated one-way data flow between the state of an existing physical object and a digital object') and lists specific methodologies like RFLP, OOSEM, SPES, and SMArDT mentioned in the background section. The mention of component interfaces and connectors in ADLs such as ACME and AADL is also supported by the text.

The extraction correctly identifies the motor type (permanent magnet synchronous), gear mechanisms (gear transmission, cycloidal gear), controller (Siemens SINUMERIK 840D sl), and input types (position, speed, acceleration). It also correctly notes safety constraints regarding the working range. However, the mention of 'precise addressing mechanisms' is a slight extrapolation not explicitly found in the text, which only states that axes 'can be controlled separately'. Supported by quotes: 'first axis is driven by a permanent magnet synchronous motor', 'The robot is controlled by the Siemens SINUMERIK 840D sl', 'safety cell of the robot restricts the maximum working range'.

The extraction states 'Not in Document', but the source documents explicitly describe the sensing infrastructure. Section III mentions 'two position encoders per drive' (a motor encoder and a secondary encoder), and Section IV.A mentions access to 'time series of all sensors' including 'input current' and 'output angle'.

The extraction accurately describes the integration of analytical and data-driven models, the validation method (comparing reference vs. simulated data), and the enforcement of data traceability. The mention of 'systematic error detection and correction mechanisms' is partially supported by the goal of improving accuracy and continuous identification, but the specific phrase 'embedded in the verification process' is a slight embellishment not explicitly detailed. Supported by quotes: 'combine analytical (white-box) and data-driven (black-box) friction models can be effectively combined with live data', 'reference values and simulated values which we compare against the reference values', 'data... remains fully traceable to its origin and method of computation'.

block_4_judge.reasoning: The extraction accurately describes the one-way data flow, specific sensor data types (input current, output angle), and the use of MQTT. This is strongly supported by the text: 'an automated one-way data flow between the state of an existing physical object and a digital object' and 'We have access to time series of all sensors, which are streamed via MQTT. These are the input current and the output angle.'

The extraction correctly identifies the lack of control loops back to the physical system and the passive nature of the digital shadow. Supported by the text: 'In practical terms, this means that a digital shadow functions as a passive entity: it neither operates autonomously nor pushes data back to the physical system.'

The extraction accurately notes the use of MQTT for streaming sensor data and mentions the dashboard visualization. It correctly identifies that specific network infrastructure details (topology, bandwidth, latency, security) are not provided in the text. The source confirms: 'streamed via MQTT' and 'visualization in a dashboard'.

The extraction states 'Not in Document,' and the source indeed lacks specific information regarding hosting infrastructure. The text mentions Asset Administration Shells (AAS) and dashboards but does not specify hosting platforms (cloud/edge/on-premise), computational requirements, or scalability mechanisms.

It focuses on the implementation for a specific use case. Therefore, the claim that this information is not in the document is accurate.

SPES... or SMArDT') and lists the ADLs (ACME, AADL, MontiArc, SysML IBDs) used for architecture ('ACME, AADL, MontiArc... or SysML IBDs'). The characteristic is marked as 'Not in Document'.
